coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent turn-off/on method for detection of Cu2+and oxalate using carbon dots as fluorescent probes in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shengrui
Wang, Qin
Tian, Guanghui
Ge, Hongguang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by the nitric acid oxidation of activated carbon. The experimental measurements showed that the resulting particles exhibited an average diameter of 50nm and tunable emission wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was effectively quenched by copper ion (Cu2+) (turn-off) and restored reversibly by the addition of oxalate (turn-on). Based on this phenomenon, an “off–on” approach for detection of Cu2+ and oxalate using CDs as fluorescent probes was performed. The quantitative detection of Cu2+ ranged from 1.0×10−5 to 9.0×10–5 mol/L was allowed, and the concentrations of oxalate in the range of 1.0×10−5–7.0×10–5 mol/L exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient R 2=0.9945. Furthermore, the detection limit for oxalate was as low as 1.0×10−6 mol/L, and the influences of 14 kind potential interference ions on oxalate determination were examined.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Oxidize
Luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of the CDs; (B) emission spectra of the CDs excited at different wavelengths.
(A) Emission spectra of CD solution mixed with Cu2+; (B) Stern–Volmer plots of CDs fluorescence intensity quenching by Cu2+; [Cu2+] =1.0×10−5–9.0×10−5 mol/L.

(A) Emission spectra of the CDs (a), CDs mixed with Cu2+ (i), and CDs–Cu2+ system mixed with oxalate (b–j); (B) a curve of linearity between the ΔF and [oxalate]. [CDs]=0.10mg/mL, [Cu2+]=1.0×10−4 mol/L; [oxalate]=1.0×10−5–7.0×10−5 mol/L.
Effects of coexisting ions on determination of oxalate.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent turn-off/on method for detection of Cu2+and oxalate using carbon dots as fluorescent probes in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong 723000, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by the nitric acid oxidation of activated carbon. The experimental measurements showed that the resulting particles exhibited an average diameter of 50nm and tunable emission wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was effectively quenched by copper ion (Cu2+) (turn-off) and restored reversibly by the addition of oxalate (turn-on). Based on this phenomenon, an “off–on” approach for detection of Cu2+ and oxalate using CDs as fluorescent probes was performed. The quantitative detection of Cu2+ ranged from 1.0×10−5 to 9.0×10–5 mol/L was allowed, and the concentrations of oxalate in the range of 1.0×10−5–7.0×10–5 mol/L exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient R 2=0.9945. Furthermore, the detection limit for oxalate was as low as 1.0×10−6 mol/L, and the influences of 14 kind potential interference ions on oxalate determination were examined.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+) as the third most abundant transition metal ion in the human body is essential in many biological reactions [1]. The content of Cu2+ in the body affects many physiological functions, such as enzyme activity and inhibit cell metabolism [2]. On the other hand, knowledge of the oxalate level in blood and urine is important in many serious clinical disorders, such as urinary stone disease, kidney lesions and pancreatic insufficiency [3]. Thus, accurate measurement of oxalate plays a pivotal role in medical, agricultural, and environmental sciences [4]. There are various methods for Cu2+ and oxalate determination such as inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [5], colorimetric [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], fluorescent sensing and biocompatible nanomaterials [8,9]. Among all these methods, the approaches using biocompatible nanomaterials are sensitive, selective, low-cost and environment-friendly.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been actively pursued and well applied in biosensing, and many scientific papers about metal ions and biological molecules sensing based on CDs have been reported [10]. Qu et al. performed CDs as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices [11]. A CDs-based fluorescence turn-on method for DNA was presented by Zhang et al. [12].

In this work, “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and oxalate using CDs as fluorescent probes was reported. The determination of Cu2+ was based on its quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. In the case of oxalate, there are four oxygen atoms that can coordinate to Cu2+ and form coordination compounds. Thus, the competitive interaction between Cu2+, oxalate and CDs could remove Cu2+ from the surface of CDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs recovered sharply. The fluorescence quenching by Cu2+ and the subsequent fluorescence recovery by oxalate made it possible to quantitatively detect Cu2+ and oxalate.

Chemicals and reagents: Activated carbon, Tris (tris(hydroxymethyl)-amino-methane) and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Standard stock solutions of anions and cations were prepared by dissolving spectral pure grade chemical reagents in double-distilled water with the addition of hydrochloric acid. Dialysis bags (MW cutoff=8kDa) were purchased from Millipore Corporation (Billerica, USA). Tris (0.2molL−1)–HCl (0.1molL−1) buffer solution was used to keep the pH of the solutions at 7.40.

Instruments and apparatus: The microstructures of the CDs were examined using a JSM-6701F scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Japan). All fluorescence spectra were recorded using a F97Pro spectrofluorophotometer (LengGuang Industrial Co., Ltd. of Shanghai, China) using 10/10nm slit widths.

Preparation of the CDs: 1.2g activated carbon was added into 240mL HNO3 (5mol/L), and refluxed at 125°C for 72h. The black suspension was centrifuged at 4000rpm for 30min, and the brownish-yellow supernatant was collected and neutralized by NaOH. Next, it was dialyzed against doubly distilled water through a dialysis bag for 3d. Finally, the brownish-yellow solution containing the CDs was collected and saved for analysis. Stock solution of the CDs (1.0mg/mL) was prepared in distilled water and stored at 4°C.

Metal ion titrations: The concentration of CDs was diluted to 0.10mg/mL with a Tris buffer solution. 1.0mL CDs (0.10mg/mL) was titrated by adding the Cu2+ ion solution at concentrations ranging from 1.0×10−5 to 9.0×10−5 mol/L.

Detection of oxalate: The concentration of CDs was diluted to 0.10mg/mL with a Tris buffer solution. 1.0mL CDs (0.10mg/mL) was firstly titrated by adding 10μL 1.0×10−2 mol/L Cu2+ working solution (After many experiments, the data of fluorescence recovery would be the best when the concentration of Cu2+ is 1.0×10−4, which were not shown in this paper), and then titrated by successive additions of a 1.0×10−3 mol/L of oxalate solution.

SEM and spectra measurements: The formation of CDs was confirmed by SEM measurement, which is shown in Fig. 1A. The size dispersity of the CDs could be seen to be relatively small, and the average diameter of the CDs was found to be approximately 50nm. Earlier works showed that CDs exhibited tunable excitation and emission wavelengths [13]. In this work, the CDs were excited over a wide range (from 300 to 580nm), and the emission spectra were shown in Fig. 1B. It could be observed that the CDs emitted at similar wavelengths when excited from 300 to 440nm, and the emission wavelengths of CDs changed from 543 to 602nm when excited from 470 to 580nm. When the maximal fluorescence intensity of the CDs was achieved, the excitation and emission wavelengths were 470 and 543nm, respectively.

Fluorescence sensing of Cu
2+ : After thermal refluxing in concentrated HNO3, the CDs are functionalized with carboxylic/carbonyl moieties on the particle surface [14,15]. When the Cu2+ was added into the system, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenching because the interaction between Cu2+ and carboxylic groups would undermine the stability of the luminescence properties of surface functional groups and lead to the aggregation of CDs (shown in Fig. 2A). The quenching of the fluorescence intensity by the Cu2+ could be described using the Stern–Volmer equation:
(1)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[
Cu
2 +
]
where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity without Cu2+, F is the fluorescence intensity observed in the presence of the Cu2+ and K SV is the Stern–Volmer (conditional stability) constant. The typical Stern–Volmer plot is shown in Fig. 2B, and a calibration curve of
F
0
/ F
= 1.033 + 7.40 ×
10
4
[
Cu
2 +
] was obtained, allowing the quantification of Cu2+ in the range of 1.0×10−5 to 9.0×10–5 mol/L.

Detection of oxalate in aqueous solution: As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was recovered sharply because the addition of oxalate anion may set up a second equilibrium between the oxalate and the Cu2+, which perturbed the CDs–Cu2+ equilibrium and resulted in changes in the complex. Fig. 3B shows that a calibration curve of Δ F = − 3.74 + 13.16 ×
10
5
[ oxalate ] (R 2=0.9945) was obtained, allowing the quantification of oxalate in the range of 1.0×10−5 to 7.0×10–5 mol/L (F 2 and F 1 were the fluorescence intensity of CDs–Cu2+ common composite system with or without the oxalate, respectively). Statistical analysis revealed a detection limit of oxalate as low as 1.0×10–6 mol/L. The precision expressed as the relative standard deviation (RSD%), obtained from a series of 11 standard samples each containing 4.0×10−5 mol/L of oxalate, was 1.35%.

Effects of coexisting ions: The selective detection of oxalate was further estimated. When the concentration of oxalate was 4.0×10−5 mol/L, the effects of 14 other kinds of cations and anions on the fluorescence response of CDs–Cu2+ were investigated. The effects of excess of 50-fold the Cl−, Br−, SCN−, and Zn2+, 25-fold the
ClO
3
−
,
NO
3
−
,
BrO
3
−
, K+, and Na+ ions, and 20-fold the Ba2+,
SO
4
2 −
,
SO
3
2 −
,
CO
3
2 −
and
SiO
3
2 −
on the determination of oxalate were investigated. As shown in Table 1, the system had significant interference from Ba2+,
SO
4
2 −
,
SO
3
2 −
,
CO
3
2 −
and
SiO
3
2 −
, which may be because the Ba2+ ion would coordinate with oxalate and those other anions would coordinate with Cu2+. However, other 9 kinds of ions did not interfere the determination of oxalate, which demonstrated that the chemosensing ensemble had a relatively high selectivity toward oxalate.

The detection of Cu2+ was performed because of its quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while the oxalate could be detected due to the binding preference of oxalate toward Cu2+ and the fluorescent recovery effect. Furthermore, 14 other kinds of cations and anions were selected to estimate the selective determination of oxalate in aqueous solution. The approach presented is facile, rapid, low cost, stable, less time-consuming, and environmental-friendly, and the results presented here may pave a way for the construction of highly selective and sensitive nanoprobes for Cu2+ and oxalate in biological or environmental samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the Startup Project of Shaanxi University of Technology (SLGQD13-3), the Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21373132) and the Society Progress Project of Shaanxi Province of China (2012K19-03-03).
